content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I’m looking for movies that are really unique. Movies that can’t be compared to anything else. Whether it’s because of the visuals, plot, acting, editing or whatever it is

**Score:** 93
**Body:** I prefer horror/thriller movies and hate anything romance. But I’m okay with any genre (other than romance obviously), any  language and any era.

I just want a movie that’s very different from anything else and is its own unique thing. Thank you

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai, Time Bandits
2. Being John malcovich
3. Brazil
4. Sorry to Bother You (2018)
  - I said, "it can't possibly get any weirder than this!" while I was watching it. Five times. I said that five times. 
5. Timecrimes (2007) 
6. Everything Everywhere All At Once
7. Häxan (1922)

Titane (2021)

Poor Things (2023)

Bugonia (2025)
8. Delicatessen. House. The butcher boy. Last and first men.
9. Being There starring Peter Sellers

## 2. Movie suggestion for me and my son? Our cat died last night.

**Score:** 41
**Body:** Hi friends,

My son (13yo) and I had to put our cat to sleep last night. It is always heart shattering to lose a pet (family member 🥹), and this one was a shock - he was only 8 years old and had no prior health issues. Not enough time. 

We’re doing the mourning rituals - watching funny videos of our cat and laughing, ugly crying, talking openly about the ebbs and flows of the grief roller coaster. Eating ice cream. 

So, today is a really hard grief day because it’s our first day without him, we’re so aware of his absence.  

It’s hard to find the movie sweet spot for a day like today, I’m hoping you guys can help us.  💔🙏🏻

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Flow (2024) or Wild Robot (2024).
  - Wild robot for sure
2. 

Kiki's Delivery Service if you want a movie with a cat



My Neighbor Totoro if you want a movie without a cat
3. Not Pet Sematary
  - 😂 I needed that, thank you 🙏🏻 
4. I'm so sorry for your loss. I'm a cat mum and understand how hard this hits. 

I love a got pixar movie 

The secret life of pets ( Kevin Hart as the voice of snowball 🤣). 

The Wild Robot.

Babe is one of my feel good films. It covers everything. Friendship, overcoming adversity, community, love ( the spider ❤️). 

Wonder 

Fly away home 

Soul

Migration

Any Wallace and Gromit film 

Sending hugs 🐾


5. If I’m wrong you’ll know before even loading it up (e.g., no animals at all), but fwiw, when I was last in your heartbroken shoes (and I’m truly so sorry), I resisted my sister pushing for [Homeward Bound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homeward_Bound:_The_Incredible_Journey?wprov=sfti1) but gave in out of exhaustion and it turned out that for us it was… perfect. Beautiful. Arguably profound. I’d be totally happy to explain how/why if it would help at all, I just don’t want to babble on here if you’re looking for a much different gear. I’ll be thinking of you, and so glad you have each other.
6. Flow
7. Big Fish has really helped me in times of grief. Sending you both so much love!
8. Princess Bride

## 3. Just some plain fun movie to chill?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** After watching so many surreal, intense, artsy, old, black & white movies i just want to watch some good hollywood slob. What are some fun movies that i can just my brain off and enjoy? I just want a good time and watch things unfold. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Mummy (1999)

2. The Blues Brothers 
3. Tag and Game Night
4. Galaxy Quest!
5. History of the World Part I 
  - OMG This!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
6. Walk Hard: The Dewey Cox Story
7. Young Frankenstein

The Jerk

Bill & Ted’s Excellent Adventure

The Adam Project

The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
8. This Is the End

The World's End 
9. National Lampoon's Animal House

 Caddyshack

Blazing Saddles

Fast Times at Ridgemont High 

Trading Places

Airplane!

## 4. Movies with the silliest/wackiest plot imaginable

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Something very odd, obscure and precise such as "A man has to travel back in time to take a dump in a public restroom in order to prevent his grandma from choking her baked beans on toast". 

Don't ask me how I came up with that. I couldn't even give you the answer. 

I want the wackiest plot imagineable. Go crazy with it! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Adventures of Baron Munchausen 
2. Freddy Got Fingered (2001)
3. Swiss Army Man

Happiness of the Katakuris 
4. Dogma - by Kevin Smith. 

Two angels, played by Ben Affleck and Matt Damon, who have been cast out of heaven to earth ,  decide they have found a way to get back into heaven. 
Also features Alan Rickman as the Angel Metatron and Alanis Morissette as God. 
And there are the 13th disciple and the last descendant of Jesus Christ ( don’t ask.)

Well worth watching.  
5. Being John Malkovich
6. Pee-wee's big adventure
7. Dude Where's my Car
8. I dunno, here's some wacky flicks 

Hundreds of Beavers 

Funky Forest 

It's a Mad Mad Mad Mad World 

The 5000 Fingers of Dr T 
9. Crank and Crank 2
10. Rubber, it's about a tyre that goes on a killing spree 

## 5. Can somebody suggest any movies about isolation that are not horror?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Hey, dawgs! I have been looking for movies where the main character is isolated or cut off from the world but it is not straight up horror. more psychological or drama focused. I want something that really leans into loneliness and atmosphere. could be survival, space, remote locations, whatever. just not full on monster stuff. leave your thoughts on this, will for surely consider it hehe, thanks in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “WILSON!!!”
2. castaway
3. The Martian (Ridley Scott, 2015)
4. Cast Away (2002) - stranded on a beach

Moon (2009) - alone on the moon

Inside (2023) - stuck in a house
  - Moon is is absolutely brilliant!
5. Into the Wild 
6. Swiss Army Man
7. 127 hours
8. Possibly Jeremiah Johnson 
9. I think we’re alone now (2018)

With Peter Dinklage. A unique take on post-apocalypse style, different and I enjoyed that. Other than that I didn’t think it was a super great movie but I did enjoy the experience.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name a film that feels less like entertainment and more like an emotional undertaking

**Score:** 488
**Body:** Is there a movie in your life that requires emotional preparation? Not something you casually press play on, but something you decide to experience. In other words, something you’ve got to “get your head right” for before you watch it.

From the beginning, movies have been capable of pulling the strongest emotions from audiences. From terror and fright in horror films to heartbreak and anguish in dramas, cinema has always been able to tug at some of my deepest feelings. For me, the most powerful films are often the sad ones, full of loss, tragedy, or history at its darkest. Stories I have to steady myself for.

For me it’s Lady Sings the Blues (1972). The graphic depictions of heroin addiction, racism, and the brutal beating death of “Piano Man” played by Richard Pryor hit me hard. I was way too young to watch that movie back in ‘72, and even now, every time I revisit it, it’s an emotional battle for me.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The road (2009)

Come and see (1985)
  - Skimmed through The Road as a kid because some older kids described some scenes for shock value. Listened to the audiobook at work. My wife had just given birth to our first child. 

I was on a forklift with one earbud at the end. I had to go to the bathroom to cry.

"If he is not the word of God God never spoke."
2. Grave of the Fireflies

![gif](giphy|hw5rhsIvk2lCE)
3. Requiem for a Dream, The Holy Mountain, and The Diving Bell and The Butterfly. 
4. I’m gonna go less dark and say Arrival. 
5. Love schindler's list. 
  - It took a terrible emotional toll on Spielberg.
6. Dear Zachary
7. The Pianist, although it entertains too.
8. Manchester by the sea

## 2. Name Your Favorite Donald Sutherland film and performance

**Score:** 326

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oddball in Kelly's Hero's.  Woof, woof!!
  - Why don't you knock it off with them negative waves?
2. Invasion of the Body Snatchers, Animal House 🍑
  - ![gif](giphy|cOmGhBSFUq6lr8nSUO)
3. Hawkeye Pierce in M\*A\*S\*H.
4. Johan von Wolfhaus, Beerfest.
5. Ronald Bartel the pyromaniac from Backdraft. “Burn it all” 
6. ![gif](giphy|ebVRVrOGmmhSU)
7. ![gif](giphy|3oKIPASjlHZsp5JRN6)

This is my job!
8. ![gif](giphy|3oEjHNB4SNN1WhcESs|downsized)

"To a New Yorker like you, a hero is some type of weird sandwich, not some nut who takes on three Tigers."

## 3. Remembering Catherine O’Hara on what would’ve been her 72nd birthday. Do you have a favorite character she played?

**Score:** 236
**Body:** I’ll start. Kate McAllister in Home Alone and Aunt Ann in the Temple Grandin biopic. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moira Rose forever and always 🤍

![gif](giphy|jUDvU0mBA9trNjOV3e|downsized)
2. She was great in Schitts Creek if you haven't watched it.
3. Lola Hetherton
  - "I love you Sammy Maudlin. I want to bear your children"
4. ![gif](giphy|eo43ChlcUBIYyNONcq|downsized)

RIP
5. Home alone remains such a classic 
  - The first two of course!
6. She played a few moms.

Other than my favorite, Kate MacAllister, she was hilarious, as Delia Deetz in Beatlejuice. 
7. https://preview.redd.it/78fnv447h3ng1.jpeg?width=181&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=9450fd2d106dead9bf5bc888feeb58c86713b878

She may have had more famous roles, but she'll always be Lola Heatherton (a Dusty Springfield type lounge singer) from SCTV to me.
8. Moira, no I mean, Lola...no I meant Cookie... and that scene where she's hammered in the restaurant in Waiting for Guffman!!! Such a treasure. So many memorable characters.  A beautiful and talented lady.

## 4. What's the best documentary you've ever seen?

**Score:** 219

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Act of Killing. It was planned as a standard documentary about a genocide in Indonesia, travelling around, interviewing different people etc but then the actual genocidal war criminals themselves interpreted this film crew as being there to glorify them, and they voluntarily helped make the documentary, including reenactments of their crimes.
2. King of Kong.  It took a subject that had at best my cursory interest and made it riveting.
3. Grizzly Man - just perfect and terrifying and funny and beautiful and sad all in one 
4. Icarus. 

Started off as a low stakes doping experiment for an amateur cyclist.

Winds up blowing the top off of Russian state-sponsored doping with direct implication of pitons involvement. Wild escalation. 
5. The act of Killing
6. Dear Zachary
7. I really liked Wild Wild Country. Very well made with a LOT of real images and stuff. I know is biased af but still amazing to watch.
8. Restrepo
9. I once saw a documentary on marijuana. That’s how I’m going to watch all documentaries from now on.
10. Jiro Dreams of Sushi

## 5. Have you seen the movie "Filth"? What did you think of it?

**Score:** 192

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It made me think that McAvoy never got half of the attention he deserves
  - This is my exact answer
2. McAvoy at his absolute best. Deliciously dark movie, a great adaptation of a fucked up novel
3. #**BRUCE!**

I've never read the book by Irvine Welsh (of **Trainspotting** fame) but McAvoy sells this like a motherfucker.

Despite Bruce Robertson being a vile, reprehensible shite of a human being, McAvoy manages to get you to want to spend time in his company and makes you share in the pain on Bruce's almost inevitable downfall.

I'm not sure of another actor who could have pulled off Bruce as well as McAvoy does and it's another reminder of what a fucking fantastic actor he is.
4. I dont wanna hear anything about Split, this is by FAR Mcavoy's best.
5. Great film.  It is actually a bit exhausting watching it with all the debauchery lol.
6. Book spoiler alert


>! There is a tapeworm in the book, a central character that is controlling him and influencing his urges and actions. It's a big miss in the film unfortunately. However, I'm not sure how they would have filmed and accommodated such a storyline and I can understand why they left it out. !<


Thus, It was a good film, but could have been much better.
7. Best acting performance of all time imo.
8. I’M FRANK SIDEBOTTOM
9. "This nation brought the world television, the steam engine, golf, whiskey, penicillin, and of course, the deep-fried Mars bar. It is great being Scottish. We're such a uniquely successful race"

So much venom from the deep fried mars bar line. love it. 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Rewatched Zone of Interest. Am I crazy to think Hedwig and Klaus “enjoy” or at least “find comfort in” knowing Jewish prisoners are far worth off than they are?

**Score:** 85
**Body:** Everyone talks about pointed indifference when talking about this film. Rightfully so. That’s probably the case for Rudolph and Hedwig’s mother. Idk if the same can be said for Hedwig and Klaus (the oldest son). I recently learned this thing called Downward Social Comparison, a coping mechanism where individuals compare themselves to those who are perceived as worse off to boost self-esteem, increase satisfaction, and improve mood. I think Hedwig and Klaus finds comfort in being reminded of what’s going on on the other side of the wall. Am I crazy?

Why do I bring up Klaus? There is a scene where he locks up his brother and he is getting satisfaction from doing so. Downward social comparison it is.

Overall excellent film. White hot scenes where girl hides apples are artsy yet metaphorical.

Sorry if my English isn’t great. I was in U.S. for a year but I am native Japanese so forgive me.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's been a while since I've seen it but I don't think the scene with Klaus locking up his brother reads as an example of downward social comparison, but more how the Holocaust is seeping into the lives of the family in insidious ways, like the ash falling on the house. Klaus mirrors what he sees and hears is happening to the Jews next door.

I think Klaus feels good because children (and adults) have sadistic impulses and it feels good to exercise that power. I think a lot of people with older siblings have the experience of being tormented by them - the point is how this torment is now inflected by the grammar of the Holocaust. The whole film plays on this idea of ordinary horror or banal evil - they're a (upper?) middle-class family just like you and me, with birthday parties and concerns about having to travel for work and family affairs and wanting to move up socioeconomically, until the director shows a scene like Klaus 'gassing' his brother, or ups the sounds of screaming in the background, or shows the women rifling through the possessions of other women that are now likely dead. 

Maybe there's also a sense that the horror of what is going on next door, the ash that sustains their beautiful gardens and provides for their family, is corrupting their lives too. Like they're enriched by the Nazi regime but at the expense of some profound loss - the child that can't sleep at night, the grandmother who is too afraid to speak openly with their child, the infidelitous father who daydreams about the logistics of mass murder, the child who can't breathe, the father's nausea when confronted by a vision of future Holocaust museums etc.

Idk this is quite rambling, but the scene reminds me more of kids playing cowboys and Indians when I was young than anything. 
  - I don’t disagree. But why do you think they have sadistic impulses? I think Klaus suffers from anxiety because his father is not present and he feels weak. His sadistic impulse is to lock up his brother and make his brother feel the anxiety so that he knows his brother is perhaps worse off than he is during Rudolph’s absence. I think that is downward social comparison. Just my opinion.
    - I think it's just plain old kids imitating what they see around them and since they're literally growing up next to an extermination camp they emulate things they've seen or heard about in their day to day.
      - That's something I've pondered since seeing this film in the theatre: how much are we to infer the kids *did* see or hear (or hear talk of)? The scene with Klaus mimicking the sound of hissing gas genuinely shocked me, because where did he get that level of information? The gassing in particular seems historically to be something that was only really grasped by anyone outside of the camps much later in the war (Rudolf obviously counting among those, but one wonders how much detail he'd ever speak of it within earshot of his kids). So the movie was jarring in that way, because I guess I never considered that the Nazis' children had that level of explicit knowledge about the goings-on beyond the walls.


I might just be experiencing a kind of schism in my thoughts because I watched the documentary THE COMMANDANT'S SHADOW fairly recently, and hearing Höss's now elderly kids deny that they ever knew or suspected anything whatsoever was just so unexpected when compared to Glazer's expression. I don't want to say too much about the Höss family's thoughts in case anyone here decides to watch the doc - I recommend it as an interesting side quest to ZONE - but it was fascinating to watch the very different coping mechanisms in the later generations. 
        - I mean they were educated in a Nazi school system / society I imagine, so they wouldn't have been averse to the idea of Jewish people being subhuman and subsequent gassing or ovens being appropriate. I'm sure living right beside the camp with the ovens blazing all night they figured things out even if those weren't mentioned.  Sure the entire movie is about people pretending everything is normal and that nothing bad is happening next door even though it's plain as day. Impossible to say if they are lying or not about whether they knew what was happening though I guess, we'll never really know. It wouldn't benefit the kids to say they knew what was happening though right? 
Thanks for the doc recommendation, goin to go on that side quest now !
2. I just want to mention this... Jonathan Glazer explicitly linked the themes of the film to the current conflict in gaza and israel, he stated that the film were intended to "reflect and confront us in the present," and argued that the it's not about "what they did then," but "what we do now." By focusing on the perpetrators' ability to compartmentalize their lives, he draws a parallel to how contemporary societies can maintain normalcy while a humanitarian crisis—specifically the dehumanization of palestinians in gaza—occurs in such close proximity.
  - 100%. I feel sorry for those suffering in Gaza due to the atrocities but probably not empathetic enough. I say to myself “But what can I do?” and the film calls me out for making an excuse and keeping normalcy.

All I am saying is although this is not the case for me, I think there are people who “finds comfort” in seeing atrocities being committed and it is somewhat naive to conclude that as them “keeping normalcy”. There are a lot more evil people than we like to think.
  - Still remember that bellend director
Of son of Saul criticising glazers wonderful speech.

  - >specifically the dehumanization of palestinians in gaza

I believe Glazer also included the Israeli victims and kidnapped on October 7th as victims of dehumanization.

## 2. What am I missing about OBAA?

**Score:** 27
**Body:** I’ve seen it twice now. Incredibly well-made film top-to-bottom. I’m not an expert, but the acting, cinematography, production design, editing, etc. all seem top-notch. It was funny, slick, and engaging to watch. 

The problem for me is that great art is supposed to be more than well-made. It is supposed to make you think and feel long after the credits roll. This movie really falls short there for me and I’m trying to understand what all these rave reviews see that I don’t.

OBAA has some interesting things to say about parenting, middle age, etc., but those thematic threads play second fiddle to clumsy political gesturing that felt more like a freshmen sociology major arguing with their dad than a best picture. 

There will be blood draws compelling parallels between capitalism and religion as foundational forces of the U.S. Bugonia sneaks up on you with the idea that out-of-touch elites and angry fringes don’t have to be evil to do evil. In Sinners, Mary is mixed race to give voice to people at the edge of the black experience and show the unfortunate but understandable appeal of “color blind” rhetoric to white-passing mixed folks. In OBAA, Willa is mixed race as a plot device. 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What you may be missing is revealed within the title “one battle after another”. 

From the inception of the Declaration of Independence to the Civil War, into Reconstruction and then into the civil rights era, America has always in turmoil. I think what Paul is trying to say is that the country has never fully healed and is in a perpetual state of conflict. I don’t think he’s endorsing anything *really* in the movie other than that people will struggle and rebel, some for tyranny and some against. 

Like Eddington it’s going to be a Rorschach test for many in the audience, but I think Paul is more open to interpretation than Aster is. 

2. Why do you say that the parentage thread is secondary to the political stuff? The whole movie is primarily all about legacy and what you leave behind. The political stuff is just a backdrop to it all. 

It doesn’t have anything positive to say about the “freedom fighters.” They accomplished absolutely 0. Their legacy was nothing. 

It is interesting to read that about Mary’s character but disregard Willa’s race as a plot device. Willa being mixed race is important because it signals that, despite anyone’s proximity to whiteness, they aren’t safe in a white supremacist world. They are still black. They are still tainted.

I think too many people are trying to figure out what the move is saying politically and are just saying culture war this, culture war that. The reality is that it doesn’t have anything new or deep to say politically. The politics is a backdrop. The primary thread is parentage and where you come from. 

if you look at it through the lens of legacy, about what you do with your life, what you strive to leave behind for others, then it’s a much more interesting film. Even Perfidia’s last letter to her daughter is about how wrong she was — the freedom, the fight, all of that didn’t matter in the end to her, and she still thinks about her daughter every day and wants to meet her. Read literally, she wants to see the only impact she was able to have in the world. 

The most glaring part is that Perfidia asks Willa to continue ‘the fight’ and the scene is Willa leaving to go to a protest. But the closure of the story is that Perfidia was utterly useless and has no legacy. Perfidia may have been her blood mother just like Lockjaw was her blood father, but she is not their legacy.
  - >It doesn’t have anything positive to say about the “freedom fighters.” They accomplished absolutely 0. Their legacy was nothing.

They free an entire detention center at the start of the movie. Benicio del Toro runs a seemingly successful 'latino Hariett Tubman situation' that involves most of his town and family that was at the very least inspired by the French 75 if not a direct offshoot. The movie ends by having Willa receive a message from the network about organized resistance and her father watching proudly as she goes off to continue the work he did as a younger man, indicating that despite all the setbacks they kept going because they believe in the work they do.

The movie is honest and clear-eyed about the French 75, how their tactics could be counter-productive, how they can fall victim to performative bureaucracy and identity politics, how individual members may not always have noble goals, but also how the enemy they are up against is infinitely more powerful than them and the fact that they even manage to survive as an organization given what they are up against (a white nationlist cult of billionaires backed by the US military) is kind of miraculous in and of itself.

You are right that most of this is secondary to the main themes around family, parenthood and legacy, and I would agree that the movie isn't exactly trying to make a super nuanced political statement (other than the obvious ones like white nationalism is bad, progress is an endless struggle, protecting your family and community is always worth it), but saying the movie has nothing positive to say about the freedom fighters is pretty absurd.
    - If the film has any political message it’s that there’s a gulf between wanting to be *seen* doing something vs wanting to actually accomplish something. Exemplified by the different methods of the French 75 vs Sensei.

Notably, Sensei is helping his community *literally in the background of shots* centered on Bob. The cinematography is slyly showing us that effective action isn’t particularly flashy or cinematic.
    - Benicio is completely separate from the French 75 and shows a completely different way of organizing and resistance. His whole thing is community based action. 

They free the detention center in the beginning, and yet afterwards you see what? People still being rounded up in detention centers. 

Yes they were successful in certain actions, but completely unsuccessful in any lasting change.

By the time Willa was born, Bob was basically done with the F75. It’s clear he gave it all up. Willa picking up the mantle and continuing at the end is not an endorsement of the F75, it’s an endorsement of Bob. I personally don’t see that the film had anything positive to say about the F75 as an organization, and that’s who I was referring to as the freedom fighters. 

Edit: maybe “nothing positive” to say is too far. I guess I’ll clarify my position to say that it doesn’t glorify them and their tactics (outside of aesthetics) as much as the critiques out there are saying.
      - > They free the detention center in the beginning, and yet afterwards you see what? People still being rounded up in detention centers.

> Yes they were successful in certain actions, but completely unsuccessful in any lasting change.

I thought one of the central messages of the film was that this isn’t necessarily a failure or a reason to give up. 

Progress and revolution are a continual project—one battle after another—that passes from generation to generation, if we bother to care and to try.
      - They’re pretty explicitly connected. “The gringo coyote” is mentioned to be working with a migrant refuge in Baktan across, which is 99.9% what Benicio is running. It isn’t a coincidence that Leo is sent to Baktan Cross. Benicio also recognizes The French 75 as being legends. None of this is coincidental 
      - >Yes they were successful in certain actions, but completely unsuccessful in any lasting change.

This is very different from them doing "absolutely nothing".

Even if you saved one person, that's doing something. And if we start to think otherwise, we've lost the plot. 
        - Corny example I heard long ago, but I still like it: 

>A man was taking a walk on a lonely beach. The sun was bright and hot, and tide had gone out, stranding thousands of starfish in the sand. The man kept walking along, thinking about what a shame it was that so many starfish were going to die. He eventually came upon another man who was throwing starfish back into the water one by one. 
>
>"Why bother?" the first man asked. "It's not going to make a difference."
>
>The other man smiled as he threw another starfish back into the water and replied, "Made a difference to that one."
3. You're not going to connect with every film that is popular/well-reviewed. If you did you would be extremely boring. Every year there are a couple of films that people go nuts about that I wind up being underwhelmed by - The Holdovers is an example for me. If you are young my best advice would be to return to OBAA in like 10 years and see how, or if, it has grown on you. 

## 3. Rewatched One Battle After Another.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** A beautiful film. A thematic exploration of struggle for autonomy and personal responsibility within the context of fractured, autocratic society. The best film Paul Thomas Anderson has made imo and my personal “The Best Picture”.

I for the most part go for “slice of life independent movies with lean writing” such as Maudie or any Kelly Reichardt films but OBAA and Oppenheimer are so special that the fact that they are big budget movies doesn’t bother me at all.

I want to know if there is any film that explores similar themes OBAA does.

Sorry for poor English. I am Japanese.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You might be interested in the movie, The Battle of Algiers, it’s the movie DiCaprio’s character is watching repeatedly throughout the movie.

How to Blow Up a Pipeline could also be what you’re looking for. 

Snowpiercer is solid and a bit of a more fantastical take on the same themes.

A bit more broad, but Parasite also explores the themes around privilege, wealth, and the barriers between different societal groups in a well made, big blockbuster way

  - Thank you so much for the recommendations! I will definitely watch them! I loved Parasite and Pipeline and you are absolutely right about Parasite! I can’t wait to see The Battle of Algiers. Trailer looks amazing!
2. It’s definitely in the top half of PTA’s incredbly special filmography. 100% the Best Picture winner for me. 
By the way, your English is absolutely fantastic. It all reads absolutely fine
  - Thank you so much for the kind words! I try to listen to podcasts in English as often as I can!
3. The movie “Z” and others by Costa Gavras are closely related.

“The Secret Agent” is another recent film that’s often been compared to it. 

“The Spook Who Sat By The Door” looks like an expanded view of the earlier history in OBAA. 
  - I saw the trailers and I love it already! Thank you for the recommendations! I will watch them!
4. If you haven’t yet, watch The Secret Agent.  It’s a remarkable look at life under dictatorship in Brazil in the 70s.  It seemed a lot like where the US might be heading if the OBAA situation weren’t so specifically “American.”  Especially looking at oligarchy.  And it takes a very striking look at generational aspects of resistance, from a very different perspective.   One battle was my best movie of the year, until I saw the secret agent.

## 4. I don't see many comedy films being highly talked about (compared to other genres). What are some everyone should watch?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Confirmation bias aside, I rarely see a mainly comedic film being critically praised. I doubt there's many in any reputable critic's lists out there.

There's 87 listed as "Comedy" in Letterboxd Top 500, but most of them are focused on a different genre.

Of course this is subjective, but what are some movies that make you laugh out loud?

For me, it would be stuff like Superbad, American Pie 1, EuroTrip, Pineapple Express, etc.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Silent and pre-code comedies are among the most beloved films of all time. For the silent era pretty much all the Buster Keaton and Charlie Chaplin films are incredible. Pre-code comedies like Duck Soup, It Happened One Night, and The Thin Man always have me dying laughing. I'd also consider all the Busby Berkeley musicals from that era to basically be comedies as well, films like Footlight Parade and Gold Diggers of 1933 are hilarious.
2. Big Lebowski, This is Spinal Tap, Airplane, Naked Gun, Life of Brian, Dr. Strangelove, Election, Lost in America, What About Bob, Walk Hard, There's Something About Mary, Princess Bride, Best in Show
  - Fuck, dude. Every single one of those is the right answer.. I'm a Holy Grail man, m'self, but I won't argue Life of Brian. I don't know why more people don't know/appreciate Dr. Strangelove...
    - Dr. Strangelove is so quotable and funny. Peter Sellers is magnificent in it, and his and all the other characters are played so damn well. Perhaps a perfect satire.
    - Dr. Strangelove is legendary. Are there "film people" that don't know or appreciate it? 


I wouldn't really expect gen Z to know it, but that's just because they're still learning film, and it's getting older now.


But yeah, it's legendary, I've never heard a negative word towards it.
3. Do yourself a favor and go see “Nirvanna the band the show the movie” now in various selected theaters.

This is one of the smartest and funniest movies I’ve seen in many years. I think it will be streaming soon if it’s not playing near you.

  - It is also my nomination for the most Canadian movie ever made, I was going to go see it again this week but it's already out of the theatre where I live :( 
4. How many times do I need to type Best in Show to hit character limit? Everything Christopher Guest is a cut above most hollywood comedy but Best in Show is godly... Fred Willard is on some absolutely inhuman level no one has ever been that funny before or since.
5. I've always had a soft spot for "What's Up, Doc" directed by Peter Bogdonovich and starring Barbara Streisand and Ryan O Neal. It's well written, beautifully paced with a chase scene on the streets of San Francisco that has to be seen to be believed. It also introduces Madeline Khan.

And it kinda plays like a Coen Brothers film now.
6. Muriel's Wedding

I believe that was my introduction to Toni Collette and she was amazing then just as she's amazing now.

Adding more since I got a notification saying I didn't meet the length requirement. Here's the IMDb summary of what this film is about: A young social outcast in Australia steals money from her parents to finance a vacation where she hopes to find happiness, and perhaps love.

## 5. Billy Wilder's first Playboy interview after 1959

**Score:** 10
**Body:** ...is what I'm looking for.

In "On Sunset Boulevard", Ed Sikov writes:

> Wilder was in the finest of forms in an interview he granted to Playboy in 1960.

On [this Wikipedia page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Playboy_interviews), the only listed Billy Wilder interview is from June 1963, not from 1960.

On [Goodreads](https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/769663.Billy_Wilder), it's 1960 again:

> The interviews/articles range in quality -- a 1944 Life article and a 1960 Playboy interview are among the best.

Were there two? If not, who is wrong here?
